missing_text:
For Those Who Come After
Anna Prince Redd

ULY Twenty-fourth... Pioneer J Day in Utah...
From hamlet to city, throngs line the thoroughfares. It is a holiday, a gala day! One hundred and two years have passed since that first small band of men and three women entered the Valley of the Great Salt Lake. One hundred and two years of almost unbelievable progress!
Thousands stand sweltering in the heat, waiting for the commemorating parade, heads bared to the flag and acutely conscious of the freedoms for which it stands.
Pretense and folly are lost, for the moment, in reverence.
Eyes are alight with pride and the spark of renewed ideals. The throat tightens as the parade, a colorful and symbolic pageant, moves down the street, historically depicting the scenes and achievements of the past. And we of the present generation can but stand and watch—and measure the adequacy of the present. Handcarts... I cannot follow them down the street. I cannot see them for my tears. "Come, Come, Ye Saints" That song is the embodiment of faith and the prayers that brought our people here. The cords of restraint all but snap, for to me it is my own paren
notes: Clear continuation from 'paren' at end of Misc file to 'ts singing' at start of Art 1. This is a Pioneer Day commemorative article that flows naturally into the pioneer story that follows.
